claim: Agent skills are folders containing a skill.md file that packages procedural knowledge (workflows and processes) not just data, providing what RAG alone cannot
actionable: Move from RAG (data grounding) to skills (procedural/organizational knowledge) by creating skills folders for company workflows, step-by-step processes, and domain expertise
source_title: Are Agent Skills the New RAG?

claim: Progressive disclosure in skills protects context windows by only loading skill metadata at startup; full skill.md and referenced files load only when triggered
actionable: Design skills with minimal metadata discovery phase; only load full documentation and references when agent decides to use the skill
source_title: Are Agent Skills the New RAG?

claim: Skills can be daisy-chained within a single conversation; one agent can load multiple skills sequentially to handle complex multi-step workflows
actionable: Structure multi-step tasks as a series of chainable skills (e.g., extract → calculate → generate document) rather than creating multiple specialist agents
source_title: Are Agent Skills the New RAG?

claim: Skills work best paired with code execution sandboxes (Docker, LLM Sandbox) so agents can create artifacts, run code, and take real actions
actionable: Combine agent skills with a code sandbox environment (e.g., LLM Sandbox in Docker) so agents can execute Python, generate documents, and produce real outputs
source_title: Are Agent Skills the New RAG?

claim: Sub-agents can be used to analyze documents in isolated context so main agent doesn't get polluted with raw document content
actionable: Delegate document analysis to sub-agents with isolated context; have them extract and summarize before returning results to main agent
source_title: Are Agent Skills the New RAG?

claim: Common skill patterns include: sequential workflow orchestration, multi-tool coordination, iterative refinement, context-aware tool selection, and domain-specific intelligence
actionable: When designing skills, map your workflow to one of these patterns: sequence (step 1 → 2 → 3), multi-tool (coordinate different APIs), iterative (refine output), contextual (choose tools based on input), or domain-specific (bake in business logic)
source_title: Are Agent Skills the New RAG?

claim: Use GVisor or similar isolation layers to sandbox container-based code execution more securely than raw Docker containers
actionable: For production agent sandboxes handling untrusted code, add GVisor between the container and host kernel to prevent container escape attacks
source_title: Are Agent Skills the New RAG?
